meta.voice: AZ Law Now — data-first, contractions, no em-dashes, peer-to-peer. Same voice rules as Brendan's investigations.
meta.rules: FB has no char limit but 100-300 chars + image + auto-embedded link is the sweet spot. Always include the image (don't rely on link preview alone — FB's preview cards are inconsistent). Lead with a number where possible. Link in body, not as the only content.

posts.text:
Buckeye's own engineering report said a roundabout would cut injury crashes 41.7% at Durango and Yuma. The City picked the cheaper signalized design. 15.4% reduction.

A 26.3-point safety gap. $1.4M saved up front. Built into the next decade of crash reports.

Full investigation with the CMF table, FHWA benchmarks, and the Buckeye population-growth math:
https://azlawnow.com/investigations/buckeye-durango-yuma-roundabout-rejected/

60% of nursing home sexual abuse victims have dementia.
80% of perpetrators are caregivers.
Only 30% of incidents reach law enforcement.

A Mesa caregiver was arrested April 21 for alleged abuse of a wheelchair-bound dementia patient. The family caught it on video.

The case fits a national pattern. HB2228 is the AZ bill that closes the registry gap. Full piece with primary sources, statutory references, and what families can do:
https://azlawnow.com/investigations/grand-court-mesa-elder-abuse-hb2228/

FHWA data on signalized intersections converted to roundabouts: 46% reduction in fatal and serious-injury crashes.

Buckeye had this data in front of them at Durango and Yuma. They picked the cheaper option anyway.

The math doesn't care about public unfamiliarity. Read the investigation:
https://azlawnow.com/investigations/buckeye-durango-yuma-roundabout-rejected/

If your loved one is in an Arizona long-term care facility, three things matter immediately:

1. Pull the AZDHS inspection record. It's public.
2. Document anything that doesn't sit right. Photos, dates, behavior changes.
3. APS hotline: 1-877-767-2385.

The data on what happens when families don't catch it early is the reason HB2228 exists. Full guide with statutory references and reporting paths:
https://azlawnow.com/investigations/grand-court-mesa-elder-abuse-hb2228/

Buckeye, Arizona. 2020 population: 93,741. 2024 estimate: 114,334. That's 32.95% growth in four years. Top tier of fastest-growing US cities.

More people. More AADT. Same arterial intersections. The road geometry decisions made today carry for decades.

Why the City's choice at Durango and Yuma compounds:
https://azlawnow.com/investigations/buckeye-durango-yuma-roundabout-rejected/

Arizona gives you 180 days to file a Notice of Claim against a public entity under ARS 12-821.01.

Tempe's own grant filings admit pavement feeding into ASU is in poor condition.

And the Arizona Board of Regents publicly states ASU isn't authorized to accept your paperwork.

That's the jurisdictional trap. Full investigation with the statute verbatim, the Fong v. Phoenix (2024) ruling, and the ABOR service rule:
https://azlawnow.com/investigations/tempe-asu-pavement-180-day-claim-clock/

Arizona licensed 237 private career schools in FY25. 37 got hit with adverse actions. Nine more closed.

The federal rule behind the pressure: under 34 CFR 668.22, once a student passes the 60% point of a payment period, the school has earned 100% of their federal aid.

National precedent is settled. The Department of Education discharged $5.8B for Corinthian and $3.9B for ITT in 2022. Full investigation with the AZ tort theory and the Title IX and Clery gap:
https://azlawnow.com/investigations/arizona-career-schools-37-adverse-actions/

Arizona schools bought MERV 13 filters and told parents the air is hospital-grade.

ASHRAE 241, the post-pandemic standard, says it in plain English: air that goes around an unsealed filter doesn't get cleaned. The system performs as if a lower MERV had been installed.

EPA tightened the annual PM2.5 standard to 9 micrograms per cubic meter in 2024. The peer-reviewed record links PM2.5 to higher mortality (Di et al. 2017, NEJM), 4.2 million annual global deaths (Burnett et al. 2018, PNAS), childhood cognition decline, autism risk, and IQ loss.

No Arizona school district publishes a single PM2.5 reading from inside its own buildings. The wellness claim is mechanical. The data isn't.

Full investigation, with the ASHRAE primary text, the AZ tort frame, and the public records request templates parents can file:
https://azlawnow.com/investigations/arizona-schools-merv-13-filter-bypass/

Arizona disciplined 325 educators in 2024. That's a 491% increase from the 55 cases in 2012.

Across the 13-year archive (2012 to 2024, 1,876 cases), sexual misconduct is the largest single category at 36% (about 671 cases). Maricopa County drove 55.6% of the pipeline.

Voluntary surrender is the dominant outcome at 39%. When a parent hears an educator "voluntarily resigned," it often means a Board surrender that ends Arizona certification but produces no public hearing.

Full investigation, with what the data tells survivor families about the ARS 12-514 and 180-day ARS 12-821.01 clocks, plus the public discipline search portal:
https://azlawnow.com/investigations/arizona-325-educator-discipline-2024/

Arizona Attorney General Kris Mayes sued the federal government Friday to block a 1,500-bed federal detention facility in Surprise.

The single fact that makes this lawsuit different from a partisan press release is the address across the street.

Directly across from the proposed facility, Rinchem Company stores chlorine, hydrofluoric acid, and fluorine. Rinchem's most recent EPA Risk Management Plan was filed January 1, 2026, three weeks before the federal warehouse purchase on January 23. The AG complaint quotes that the RMP "does not consider the potential hazards incurred by the presence of a mass detention facility next door."

Three sister-state AGs filed materially similar NEPA suits earlier in 2026. Maryland won a TRO on March 11. Arizona is the fourth state on this fact pattern.

Full investigation, with the Rinchem chemical-zone frame, the NEPA + INA + Clean Air Act claims, and what comes next:
https://azlawnow.com/investigations/arizona-mayes-ice-surprise-detention-lawsuit/

ASU Preparatory Academy is Arizona's largest university-affiliated charter network. Its most recent IRS Form 990 (fiscal year ending June 2024) reports $82.4M in revenue against $87.9M in expenses. That's a $5.46M operating loss.

The network's FY21 audited consolidated financial statements, filed with the Arizona Department of Education, disclose something else. Note 6 of that audit reads: "ASU Prep leases a building used for a school site from a related party (see Note 9) under an agreement expiring September 2032." Aggregate operating-lease right-of-use asset: $11,543,739.

The FY21 audit had zero findings. Auditor issued a clean opinion. Related-party transactions are legal in nonprofit finance when properly disclosed, board-approved, and at fair-market terms. Note 9 is where the counterparty's name lives.

We pulled the audit text, mapped the federal-funds flow, and laid out the public-records path under A.R.S. 39-121.

Full investigation: https://azlawnow.com/investigations/asu-prep-related-party-lease/

In April 2025, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Eastern District of New York announced a federal settlement with Walgreens valued at up to $350 million. The allegation: Walgreens pharmacists knowingly filled millions of unlawful opioid prescriptions and Walgreens then submitted false claims for those prescriptions to federal health programs. Covered conduct: August 2012 through March 2023.

Arizona's most recent provisional 12-month opioid overdose death count, per AZDHS, is 1,659 (as of November 2024). The 2021 full-year figure was 2,846.

Which Arizona Walgreens stores filled the prescriptions covered by the settlement? The DOJ press release describes the conduct as nationwide. The store-by-store list, if it exists in the consent decree exhibits, sits in the federal docket in the Eastern District of New York. That's a PACER pull or a FOIA request.

We mapped the federal frame, the Arizona death toll, the state regulator architecture (Board of Pharmacy, DEA Phoenix), and the statutory frame (A.R.S. 32-1901, 13-3408, 36-2606).

Full investigation: https://azlawnow.com/investigations/walgreens-350m-arizona-opioid-stores/

Buckeye's own engineering report projected a roundabout would cut serious-injury crashes 41.7% at Durango and Yuma.

The City picked the cheaper signalized design: 15.4% reduction. A 26.3 percentage-point safety gap, traded for $1.4 million in concrete savings.

Full investigation with the CMF data, FHWA national context, and Buckeye's 32.95% population growth: https://azlawnow.com/investigations/buckeye-durango-yuma-roundabout-rejected/

Free to embed for editorial use — chart on the page comes with copy-paste embed code.

Arizona's Board for Private Postsecondary Education licensed 237 private career schools in fiscal year 2025. 37 got adverse actions. 9 schools closed.

Most students never see the audit-stage paperwork that follows them, until the Title IV refund math collides with a missed clinical placement.

Full investigation with the federal 60% rule, the FY25 Board numbers, and Arizona's Consumer Fraud Act stacking theory: https://azlawnow.com/investigations/arizona-career-schools-37-adverse-actions/

Free to embed for editorial use, chart on the page comes with copy-paste embed code.

51 daycare facilities cited 3 or more times across Buckeye, Goodyear, Avondale, and Surprise, 2023-2025.

Staff-ratio violations are 40% of West Valley citations. Repeat violators can operate for years before AZ DHS escalates enforcement.

Full investigation with city-by-city facility lists, AZ DHS CareCheck data, and the corrective-action timeline: https://azlawnow.com/investigations/arizona-daycare-violations/

Free to embed for editorial use.
